coredata.dc:title: A highly sensitive chemiluminescence assay for diniconazole by using CuInS2 quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Rasoulzadeh, Farzaneh
Amjadi, Mohammad
Ghorbani, Marjan
coredata.dc:description: We demonstrate here the first use of water-soluble copper indium sulfide quantum dots (CuInS2 QDs) as the chemiluminescence (CL) reagent and its application to the highly sensitive detection of diniconazole. We utilized a hydrothermal method for synthesis of mercaptopropionic acid-capped CuInS2 QDs, which were characterized by TEM image, EDS, and XRD pattern. The performance of these QDs as the CL reagent was investigated by using several oxidizing agents and found that acidic potassium permanganate (KMnO4) gave the highest CL signal. CuInS2 QDs/KMnO4 as a novel CL probe was then used for assay of diniconazole based on its unique enhancing effect. In the optimum settings, a linear calibration plot was obtained for diniconazole in the concentration range of 4.0 × 10−9 mol L−1–1.5 × 10−6 mol L−1, with a limit of detection of 1.0 × 10−9 mol L−1. The study of interferences and recoveries confirmed the suitability of the method for assay of environmental water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quantum dots
Luminescence
Pesticides
Triazole
Sensors

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Study of the chemiluminescence of CuInS2 QDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of MPA-capped CuInS2 QDs (25 mg L−1), λex = 500 nm. Inset: The structure of diniconazole.

Kinetic curve for (a) H2SO4/KMnO4, (b) QDs/KMnO4 CL system and (c) QDs/KMnO4 CL system in the presence of diniconazole (3.5 × 10−7 mol L−1). Conditions: QDs, 25 mg L−1, KMnO4, 3.0 × 10−3 mol L−1, H2SO4, 1.5 mg L−1.
Comparison of the method with previously reported methods for the determination of diniconazole.
Interferences of various coexisting species in the determination of diniconazole (1.0 × 10−7 mol L−1).

row.entry: Tolerance limit (Interferent to analyte concentration ratio)
Na+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Ni+, F-, Cl-, SO4 2-, PO4 3-

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recoveries of diniconazole from fortified water samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Mean of three measurements ± standard deviation.

head.ce:title: A highly sensitive chemiluminescence assay for diniconazole by using CuInS2 quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz 5166616471, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz 5166616471, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Stem Cell Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Stem Cell Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We demonstrate here the first use of water-soluble copper indium sulfide quantum dots (CuInS2 QDs) as the chemiluminescence (CL) reagent and its application to the highly sensitive detection of diniconazole. We utilized a hydrothermal method for synthesis of mercaptopropionic acid-capped CuInS2 QDs, which were characterized by TEM image, EDS, and XRD pattern. The performance of these QDs as the CL reagent was investigated by using several oxidizing agents and found that acidic potassium permanganate (KMnO4) gave the highest CL signal. CuInS2 QDs/KMnO4 as a novel CL probe was then used for assay of diniconazole based on its unique enhancing effect. In the optimum settings, a linear calibration plot was obtained for diniconazole in the concentration range of 4.0 × 10−9 mol L−1–1.5 × 10−6 mol L−1, with a limit of detection of 1.0 × 10−9 mol L−1. The study of interferences and recoveries confirmed the suitability of the method for assay of environmental water samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Several luminescent quantum dots (QDs) have been used in CL as a catalyst [9], energy acceptor, or luminophore. Oxidizing agents like Ce(IV), KMnO4, K3Fe(CN)6 and hypochlorite have been used to study the direct CL of some QDs. In such cases, QDs often contribute to a CL reaction as emitters through oxidation or CL energy transfer [10–13]. Most traditional QDs were synthesized with poisonous class A components (Cd, Pb, and Hg) or class B components (Se and As) that are toxic to organisms and ultimately produce serious ecological problems [10,14,15]. Recently, copper indium sulfide (CuInS2) QDs that contain less toxic elements attracted researchers’ attention as a novel class of QDs [16,17]. They are ternary I–III–VI semiconductors with some interesting properties like high absorption coefficient, size-tunable fluorescence covering whole visible up to near-infrared (NIR) spectral region, and good defect tolerance [16]. Several methods have been described for the synthesis of CuInS2 QDs, such as solvothermal, hot-injection and heating-up procedures, which most of them are performed in organic solvents [18,19]. These QDs have been used in different areas such as solar cells, drug delivery and cell imaging. There are also a limited number of reports in which CuInS2 QDs have been applied for analytical purposes. For example, Su and coworkers utilized CuInS2 QDs as a fluorescent probe for the determination of some analytes [20,21]. However, the analytical potential of these QDs has yet to be explored thoroughly.

We here used a one-pot hydrothermal procedure for preparation of water-soluble CuInS2 QDs with mercaptopropionic acid (MPA) as a capping agent. We then studied the possibility of using these QDs in the CL field for the first time. In the next step, CuInS2 QDs/KMnO4 reaction was applied as a CL probe for ultrasensitive determination of diniconazole in environmental water samples.

The CL intensities were recorded by a Berthold LUMAT LB 9507 chemiluminometer (Germany). The device had an automatic injector which applied for KMnO4 injection. UV–vis spectra were acquired by a Cary-100 spectrophotometer (Varian, Australia). Fluorescence spectra were monitored by Jasco FP-8300 spectrofluorimeter (Japan). The shape and particle size of QDs were characterized by the TEM image obtained by Leo 906 instrument (Zeiss, Germany). FTIR spectra were achieved with an FT-IR spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) was obtained with Philips PW 1730 diffractometer using Cu Ka radiation. Energy-Dispersive Spectroscopy (EDS) was performed on a MIRA III field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM) operated at 20 kV.

Deionized water was applied throughout the experiment. NaOH, KMnO4, Copper(II) chloride dehydrate (CuCl2·2H2O), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), sulfourea (CS(NH2)2) were all obtained from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). Indium(III) chloride tetrahydrate (InCl3·4H2O) and MPA were bought from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Diniconazole was kindly provided by GYAH Co. (Karaj, Iran).

MPA-capped CuInS2 QDs were prepared by a hydrothermal process [21]. Briefly, a homogeneous mixture of 0.15 mmol CuCl2·2H2O and 0.15 mmol InCl3·4H2O was prepared in deionized water (the volume was 10.5 mL). After adding 1.8 mmol MPA into the solution, yellow granules were formed. Then the solution pH was adjusted to 11.3 by adding 2.0 mol L−1 NaOH. In this step, the solution color transformed into pink. It was stirred for 10 min, and then 0.30 mmol CS(NH2)2 was added. The ratios of Cu-to-MPA and Cu-to-In-to-S were 1:12 and 1:1:2, respectively. All steps were completed at ambient temperature. The mixture was sealed into a 25 mL Teflon equipped stainless steel autoclave and heated at 150 °C for 21 h. After cooling down to ambient temperature, the yellow CuInS2 QDs were gotten by centrifugation. The precipitate was washed three times by ethanol and centrifuged. The product was dried in a vacuum oven to obtain the CuInS2 QDs powder. The working solution was prepared by dispersing 2.5 mg of the QD powder in 10 mL deionized water.

Static-injection CL measurements were carried out in a 3-mL tube. For this purpose, 100 µL of diluted QDs (250 mol L−1), 300 µL of H2SO4 (5 mol L−1) and a suitable volume of analyte solution (either sample or standard) were added into the cell. The mixture was diluted to 700 μL with distilled water. To trigger the CL reaction and record the signal versus time, 300 μL of KMnO4 (0.01 mol L−1) was injected by the automatic injector. The maximum CL signal was used for analytical purposes.

Table, spring and tap water samples were acquired from the outskirt of Tabriz (Iran). An appropriate volume of each sample was used for analysis by the above procedure without any pretreatment except a prior filtration. For validation of the assay, known amounts of diniconazole were spiked to the water samples.

The UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of CuInS2 QDs are shown in Fig. 1. The absorption spectrum shows a broad peak with a slight shoulder around 500 nm. The fluorescence emission peak is narrow and symmetrical, with a maximum at about 600 nm. Three batches of CuInS2 QDs were synthesized via a single method and their fluorescence spectra were recorded. As can be seen in Fig. S1 (Electronic Supplementary Material, ESM) the variability in the emission peak was small, which indicates the good repeatability of the preparation method. The fluorescence quantum yield was estimated to be 2% by using rhodamine B as a standard.

To identify the functional composition of the as-synthesized QDs, we obtained the FT-IR spectrum. Fig. S2A (ESM) shows the FTIR spectra of the MPA-capped CuInS2 QDs and MPA. The COOH stretching vibration (at 1568 cm−1), and C–H stretching vibration (at 2859 cm−1) indicate the MPA functional groups. The characteristic band of S–H (within 2550–2680 cm−1) did not appear in the spectrum probably because of the covalent attachment of thiol to metal atoms of the ternary QDs. So we can confirm that the surface of CuInS2 QDs has been covered by the stabilizer MPA. Chemical composition of CuInS2 QDs was determined by EDS (Fig. S2B, ESM), which revealed the nearly stoichiometric composition of Cu, In and S with no impurity. The EDS result indicated that the Cu to In molar ratio was 1:1, being consistent with the composition of CuInS2 [22].

As revealed in the TEM image (Fig. S3A, ESM), MPA-CuInS2 QDs were spherical with average sizes of 3 ± 1 nm. XRD pattern is useful for studying the phase purity and crystallinity of CuInS2 QDs. In the pattern shown in Fig. S3B (ESM), three major diffraction peaks appear around 2θ = 28, 47 and 55°, which were assigned to their (112), (204/220), and (116/312) lattice planes, respectively [14,15,22–24]. Considering the most intense peak in this pattern, it can be concluded that the crystalline structure of the CuInS2 is oriented along the (112) plane. The diffraction spectrum also evidently indicates no secondary phases, and the resultant peak positions approve the chalcopyrite structure of CuInS2 particles. Therefore, the chalcopyrite tetragonal configuration of as-synthesized MP-CuInS2 QDs could be approved. Elemental analysis from XRD spectra approves the results of EDS spectra, as no diffraction peaks of impurity parts have been identified in the QDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of the chemiluminescence of CuInS2 QDs
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the CL behavior of CuInS2 QDs, several oxidants were examined. Only with the acidic potassium permanganate solution, a considerable CL occurred (Fig. S4, ESM). With acidic solutions of periodate (IO4 -) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), almost no signal, and with acidic Ce(IV) solution, a weak signal was achieved. In the next step, chemical conditions for CL reaction were optimized to obtain the highest CL signal. The nature and concentration of acid are vital in eliciting CL form QDs by potassium permanganate. So the CL reaction was initially conducted with three common acids, HCl, H2SO4, H3PO4 in the concentration range of 0.04–2.0 mol L−1 (Fig. S5, ESM). The highest signal was produced by a potassium permanganate solution containing 1.5 mol L−1 H2SO4. The influence of QD concentration was investigated, and the results are presented in Fig. S6A (ESM). As seen, the CL intensity increased until 25 mol L−1 of QDs and remained constant at higher amounts. So 25 mol L−1 was selected as the optimum concentration. The influence of permanganate concentration in the range of 2.5 × 10−5–3.5 × 10−3 mol L−1 on the CL signal was the next parameter examined, keeping the concentration of H2SO4 and QDs fixed at 1.5 mol L−1 and 25 mg L−1 respectively. Fig. S6B (ESM) shows the result, revealing that the highest CL was achieved at 3.0 × 10−3 mol L−1 permanganate.

It was found that trace amounts of fungicide diniconazole significantly increase the CL intensity of CuInS2 QDs/KMnO4 system. Fig. 2 demonstrates the typical CL kinetic curves for this reaction with and without diniconazole. The rate of the CL reaction was quite high, and the maximum CL was achieved within 1 s after mixing the reagents. It took about 2 s for the signal to return to the baseline.

Utilizing this fact, a very sensitive CL probe was established for the assay of diniconazole. In the optimum experimental conditions ([MnO4 −] = 3.0 × 10−3 mol L−1, [H2SO4] = 1.5 mol L−1, [QD] = 25 mol L−1), the analytical performance of the probe was evaluated, and a calibration graph was constructed with 11 different concentrations of diniconazole within the range of 4.0 × 10−9 mol L−1–1.5 × 10−6 mol L−1. The limit of detection (LOD) defined as three times the signal to noise ratio was 1.0 × 10−9 mol L−1. The regression equation was ΔI = 3235.6C + 32,002, R2 = 0.998 (ΔI is equal to I-I0 with I and I0 being the CL signal in the presence and absence of diniconazole, respectively, and C is the concentration of diniconazole in 10−9 mol L−1). The method showed good precision (RSD = 2.9%) with a standard deviation (SD) of 3.8 for five replicate analyses of 5.0 × 10−6 mol L−1 diniconazole.

Comparison of the developed method for diniconazole with some other reported methods is summarized in Table 1 . As can be seen, the detection limit and linear range of our assay is better than most other methods.

Firstly, for recognizing the emitting species, we used optical filters. It was found that the emitted CL is approximately within the wavelength range of 450–600 nm, which corresponds to the wavelength of CuInS2 QDs emission. Therefore, we can propose the following simple scheme for CL:
QDs +
KMnO 4
→
QDs
∗
+ Mn
II
QDs
∗
→ QDs + h ν
~ 600
nm

In this reaction excited-state QDs (QDs*) are formed upon oxidation of QDs by permanganate, which then emit through electron-hole recombination. KMnO4 as the oxidant, may inject holes into the QDs. These holes then interact with the electrons thermally excited to the high energy band of QDs, leading to electrons–hole annihilation and CL emission [10,28]. This process can be shown as follows:
Q D
→
K M n
O 4
Q D
h
+
Q D
→
h e a t
Q D
(
e
-
)
Q D
h
+
+ Q D
e
-
→
Q D
∗
Q D
∗
→ Q D + h ν
(
600
n m )

In order to understand why the CL is enhanced by diniconazole, we studied the fluorescence spectra of QDs in the presence of this analyte. As can be seen in Fig. S7 (ESM), the fluorescence emission intensity of CuInS2 QDs increased by addition of diniconazole. This is probably due to the interaction of diniconazole molecules with the MPA-capped CuInS2 QDs. Because of the presence of a triazole group in diniconazole structure, strong Cu–N bonding with CuInS2 QDs might form. The attachment of diniconazole molecules onto the surface of CuInS2 QDs changes the surface and causes the decrease of surface deficiencies or electron traps. This exclusion of deficiency levels increases the luminescence intensity of CuInS2 QDs.

To examine the selectivity of the assay, the influence of different ions and compounds which may be present in the environmental water samples was studied on the determination of 1.0 × 10−7 mol L−1 diniconazole. The acceptable limits for interferences (relative error = 5%) are shown in Table 2 . According to this Table, most species at high concentrations only cause an insignificant change in the CL signal. These results confirm that the suggested method has sufficient selectivity for determination of diniconazole in real samples. It should be mentioned that the selectivity of the method to diniconazole is probably due to its higher affinity to QDs compared to other molecules, which is related to their chemical structures.

For evaluation of the applicability of the method, three water samples were examined. The samples were prepared by spiking with different concentrations of diniconazole and analyzed by the procedure given in Section 2.4. As can be seen in Table 3 , acceptable recoveries in the range of 95–104% were obtained. Moreover, the results were compared with a HPLC method. Statistical analysis by using paired t-test at the 95% confidence level showed that the results were in good agreement with those obtained from HPLC.

In conclusion, direct CL of water-soluble ternary CuInS2 QDs was examined by using some common oxidants, and CuInS2 QDs/KMnO4 reaction was presented as a novel CL system. The CL in this reaction supposed to be resulted from the radiative recombination of holes (inserted by oxidant) and excited-state electrons available in the QDs. Additionally, we found that the CL of this system remarkably increased by diniconazole because of their interaction with QDs. By utilizing this effect, a highly sensitive CL assay was introduced for the determination the trace quantity of diniconazole in water samples.

Farzaneh Rasoulzadeh: Investigation, Validation, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Mohammad Amjadi: Conceptualization, Supervision, Resources, Writing - review & editing. Marjan Ghorbani: Investigation, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2020.105323.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

Curr. Appl Phys.

Chem. Commun.

Biosens. Bioelectron.

Jalili, Biosensors and Bioelectronics Molecularly imprinted mesoporous silica embedded with carbon dots and semiconductor quantum dots as a ratiometric fl uorescent sensor for diniconazole, Biosens. Bioelectron. 96 (2017) 121–126. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bios.2017.04.045.

Food Chem.

Anal. Methods

Millán, Development and application of a screening method for triazole fungicide determination in liquid and fruit samples using solid-phase microextraction and HPLC-DAD, Anal. Methods. 5 (2013) 2565–2571. https://doi.org/10.1039/c3ay26433e.
